title: Why Most A/B Tests Are Lying to You
optimized_headline: Uncovering the Hidden Truths Behind A/B Testing Results
raw_excerpt:
The 4 statistical sins that invalidate most A/B tests, plus a pre-test checklist and Bayesian vs frequentist decision framework you can use Monday.
The post Why Most A/B Tests Are Lying to You appeared first on Towards Data Science.
raw_body:
The 4 statistical sins that invalidate most A/B tests, plus a pre-test checklist and Bayesian vs frequentist decision framework you can use Monday.
The post Why Most A/B Tests Are Lying to You appeared first on Towards Data Science.
speedrun: Many A/B tests may be misleading due to four common statistical errors. Issues like p-hacking and improper sample sizes can distort results, leading to incorrect conclusions. The article also discusses a checklist for pre-test preparation and contrasts Bayesian and frequentist approaches for better decision-making. Understanding these pitfalls is crucial now, as businesses rely heavily on A/B testing for data-driven decisions.
why_it_matters: Marketers and product teams could misallocate resources based on faulty A/B test results, impacting their strategies and outcomes.
Recognizing these statistical errors could lead to a broader industry shift towards more reliable testing methods, enhancing overall decision-making.

lenses.eli12: A/B tests help businesses decide which version of a product or ad performs better. However, many tests fail due to common mistakes, like using the wrong sample size or analyzing data improperly. This is like trying to judge a race by only watching the last lap. Knowing these errors can help everyone make better choices in everyday situations.
lenses.pm: For product managers, understanding the flaws in A/B testing is vital. If tests are flawed, it could lead to poor product decisions and wasted resources. By using a proper checklist and differentiating between Bayesian and frequentist methods, product teams could improve their testing accuracy and make more informed choices.
lenses.engineer: From a technical perspective, A/B tests often fall short due to issues like p-hacking and biased sampling methods. For instance, improper sample sizes can lead to skewed results, making it hard to detect true effects. Engineers should consider using Bayesian methods, which can provide more robust insights compared to traditional frequentist approaches, especially in complex scenarios.
